html:
IV

I

‚ÄúMiss Harding to see you, madam.‚Äù

Nell started. Twenty-four hours had elapsed since her interview with Vernon. She had thought it was finished. And now Jane!

She was afraid of Jane.

She might refuse to see her.

She said: ‚ÄúShow her up here.‚Äù

It was more private up here in her own sitting room.

What a long time it was waiting. Had Jane gone away again? No, here she was.

She looked very tall. Nell cowered down on the sofa. Jane had a wicked face‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe had always thought so. There was a look on her face now as of an avenging Fury.

The butler left the room. Jane stood towering over Nell. Then she flung back her head and laughed.

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt forget to ask me to the christening,‚Äù she said.

Nell flinched. She said haughtily:

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know what you mean?‚Äù

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs a family secret at present, is it? Nell, you damned little liar‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou‚Äôre not going to have a child. I don‚Äôt believe you ever will have a child‚ÅÝ‚Äîtoo much risk and pain. What made you think of telling Vernon such a peculiarly damnable lie?‚Äù

Nell said sullenly: ‚ÄúI never told him. He‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe guessed.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs even more damnable.‚Äù

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know what you mean coming here and‚ÅÝ‚Äîand saying things like this.‚Äù

Her protest sounded weak‚ÅÝ‚Äîspiritless. For the life of her she couldn‚Äôt put the necessary indignation into it. With anyone else‚ÅÝ‚Äînot with Jane. Jane had always been disagreeably clear-eyed. It was awful! If only Jane would go away.

She rose to her feet, trying to sound decisive.

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know why you have come here. If it is only to make a scene‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúListen, Nell. You‚Äôre going to hear the truth. You chucked Vernon once before. He came to me. Yes‚ÅÝ‚Äîto me. He lived with me for three months. He was living with me when you came to my flat that day. Ah! that hurts you. You‚Äôve still got a bit of raw womanhood left in you, I‚Äôm glad to see.

‚ÄúYou took him from me then. He went to you and never gave me a thought. He‚Äôs yours now if you want him. But I tell you this, Nell, if you let him down a second time, he‚Äôll come to me again. Oh! yes, he will. You‚Äôve thought things about me in your mind‚ÅÝ‚Äîturned up your nose at me as ‚Äòa certain kind of woman.‚Äô Well, because of that, perhaps, I‚Äôve got power. I know more about men than you will ever learn. I can get Vernon if I want him. And I do want him. I always have.‚Äù

Nell shuddered. She turned her face away, digging her nails into the palms of her hands.

‚ÄúWhy do you tell me all this? You‚Äôre a devil.‚Äù

‚ÄúI tell it you to hurt you! To hurt you like hell before it‚Äôs too late. No, you shan‚Äôt turn your head away. You shan‚Äôt shrink away from what I‚Äôm telling you. You‚Äôve got to look at me and see‚ÅÝ‚Äîyes, see‚ÅÝ‚Äîwith your eyes and your heart and your brain.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ You love Vernon with the last remaining corner of your miserable little soul. Think of him in my arms‚ÅÝ‚Äîthink of his lips on mine, of his kisses burning my body.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Yes, you shall think of it.

‚ÄúSoon you won‚Äôt mind even that. But you mind now.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Aren‚Äôt you enough of a woman to jib at handing over the man you love to another woman? To a woman you hate? ‚ÄòA present for Jane with love from Nell.‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù

‚ÄúGo away,‚Äù said Nell faintly. ‚ÄúGo away.‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm going. It‚Äôs not too late. You can undo the lie you told.‚Äù

‚ÄúGo away.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Go away.‚Äù

‚ÄúDo it soon‚ÅÝ‚Äîor you‚Äôll never do it.‚Äù Jane paused at the door, looking back over her shoulder. ‚ÄúI came for Vernon‚Äôs sake‚ÅÝ‚Äînot mine. I want him back. And I shall have him‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù she paused, ‚Äúunless‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

She went out.

Nell sat with her hands clenched.

She murmured fiercely: ‚ÄúShe shan‚Äôt have him. She shan‚Äôt.‚Äù

She wanted Vernon. She wanted him. He had loved Jane once. He would love her again. What had she said? ‚Äú‚Ä¶¬Ýhis lips on mine‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ his kisses burning my‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù Oh, God, she couldn‚Äôt bear it. She started up‚ÅÝ‚Äîmoved towards the telephone.

The door opened. She turned slowly. George came in. He looked very normal and cheerful.

‚ÄúHullo, sweetheart.‚Äù He crossed the room and kissed her. ‚ÄúHere I am‚ÅÝ‚Äîback again. A nasty crossing. I‚Äôd rather have the Atlantic than the Channel any day.‚Äù

She had completely forgotten that George was coming home today! She couldn‚Äôt tell him this minute‚ÅÝ‚Äîit would be too cruel. And besides it was so difficult‚ÅÝ‚Äîto burst in with the tragic news in the middle of a flow of banalities. This evening‚ÅÝ‚Äîlater. In the meantime she would play her part.

She returned his embrace mechanically, sat down and listened while he talked.

‚ÄúI‚Äôve got a present for you, honey. Something that reminded me of you.‚Äù

He took a velvet case from his pocket.

Inside, on a bed of white velvet, lay a big rose-coloured diamond‚ÅÝ‚Äîexquisite, flawless‚ÅÝ‚Äîdepending from a long chain. Nell gave a little gasp of pleasure.

He lifted the jewel from the case and slipped the chain over her head. She looked down. The exquisite rose-coloured stone blinked up at her from its resting place between her breasts. Something about it hypnotized her.

He led her to the glass. She saw a golden-haired beautiful woman, very calm and elegant. She saw the waved and shingled hair, the manicured hands, the foamy negligee of soft lace, the cobweb-silk stockings and little embroidered mules. She saw the hard cold beauty of the rose-coloured diamond.

And behind them she saw George Chetwynd‚ÅÝ‚Äîkindly, generous, deliciously safe.

Dear George, she couldn‚Äôt hurt him.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

Kisses.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ What, after all, were kisses? You needn‚Äôt think about them. Better not to think of them.

Vernon‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Jane‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

She wouldn‚Äôt think of them. For good or evil she‚Äôd made her choice. There would be bad moments sometimes, but on the whole it would be for the best. Better for Vernon too. If she weren‚Äôt happy she couldn‚Äôt make him happy.

She said gently: ‚ÄúYou are a dear to bring me such a lovely present. Ring for tea. We‚Äôll have it up here.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat will be fine. But weren‚Äôt you going to telephone to someone? I interrupted you.‚Äù

She shook her head.

‚ÄúNo,‚Äù she said, ‚ÄúI‚Äôve changed my mind.‚Äù

II

Letters from Vernon Deyre to Sebastian Levinne

Moscow.

Dear Sebastian:

Do you know that there was once a legend in Russia that concerned a ‚Äúnameless beast‚Äù that was coming?

I mention this not because of any political significance (by the way, the Antichrist hysteria is curious, isn‚Äôt it?) but because it reminded me of my own terror of ‚Äúthe Beast.‚Äù I‚Äôve thought about ‚Äúthe Beast‚Äù a great deal since coming to Russia‚ÅÝ‚Äîtrying to get at its true significance.

Because there‚Äôs more in it than just being afraid of a piano. The doctor in London opened my eyes to a great many things. I‚Äôve begun to see that all through my life I‚Äôve been a coward. I think you‚Äôve known that, Sebastian. You wouldn‚Äôt put it in that offensive way, but you hinted as much to me once. I‚Äôve run away from things.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Always I‚Äôve run away from things.

And thinking it all over now, I see the Beast as something symbolical‚ÅÝ‚Äînot a mere piece of furniture composed of wood and wires. Don‚Äôt mathematicians say that the future exists at the same time as the past?‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat we travel through time as we travel through space?‚ÅÝ‚Äîfrom a thing that is to another thing that is? Don‚Äôt some even hold that remembering is a mere habit of the mind‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat we could remember forward as well as back if we had only learnt the trick of it? It sounds nonsense when I say it, but I believe there is some theory of that kind.

I believe that there is some part of us that does know the future, that is always intimately aware of it.

That explains, doesn‚Äôt it, why we should shrink sometimes? The burden of our destiny is going to be heavy and we recoil from its shadow. I tried to escape from music‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut it got me. It got me at that concert, in the same way that religion got those people at the Salvation Army meeting.

It‚Äôs a devilish thing‚ÅÝ‚Äîor is it godlike? If so it‚Äôs an Old Testament jealous God‚ÅÝ‚Äîall the things I‚Äôve tried to cling on to have been swept away. Abbots Puissants‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Nell‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

And, damn it all, what‚Äôs left? Nothing. Not even the cursed thing itself. I‚Äôve no wish to write music. I hear nothing‚ÅÝ‚Äîfeel nothing. Will it ever come back? Jane says it will. She seems very sure. She sends her love to you, by the way.

Yours,
Vernon.

III

Moscow.

You‚Äôre an understanding devil, Sebastian. You don‚Äôt complain that I ought to have written you a description of samovars, the political situation and life in Russia generally. The country, of course, is in a bloody muddle. What else could it be in? But it‚Äôs jolly interesting.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

Love from Jane.

Vernon.

IV

Moscow.

Dear Sebastian:

Jane was right to bring me here. Point No.¬Ý1, no one is likely to come across me here and joyously proclaim my resurrection from the dead. Point No.¬Ý2, this is about the most interesting place in the world to be from my point of view. A kind of free and easy laboratory where everyone is trying experiments of the most dangerous kind. The whole world seems concerned with Russia from a purely political point of view. Economics, starvation, morals, lack of liberty, diseased and decadent children‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ etc.

But amazing things are sometimes born out of vice and filth and anarchy. The whole trend of Russian thought in art is extraordinary‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ part of it the most utter childish drivel you ever heard‚ÅÝ‚Äîand yet wonderful gleams peeping through‚ÅÝ‚Äîlike shining flesh through a beggar‚Äôs rags‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

The Nameless Beast‚ÅÝ‚ÄîCollective Man. Did you ever see that plan for a monument to the Communist Revolution? The Colossus of Iron? I tell you, it stirs the imagination.

Machinery‚ÅÝ‚Äîan Age of Machinery.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ How the Bolsheviks worship anything to do with machinery‚ÅÝ‚Äîand how little they know about it! That‚Äôs why it‚Äôs so wonderful to them, I suppose. Imagine a real mechanic of Chicago composing a dynamic poem describing his city as ‚Äú‚Ä¶¬Ýbuilt upon a screw! Electro dynamo mechanical city! Spiral-shaped on a steel disc. At every stroke of the hour turning round itself‚ÅÝ‚ÄîFive thousand skyscrapers‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù Anything more alien from the spirit of America!

And yet‚ÅÝ‚Äîdo you ever see a thing when you‚Äôre too close to it? It‚Äôs the people who don‚Äôt know machinery who see its soul and its meaning. The Nameless Beast.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ My Beast?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I wonder.

Collective Man‚ÅÝ‚Äîforming himself in turn into a vast machine. The same herd instinct that saved the race of old coming out again in a different form.

Life‚Äôs becoming too difficult‚ÅÝ‚Äîtoo dangerous‚ÅÝ‚Äîfor the individual. What was it Dostoevsky says in one of his books?

‚ÄúThe flock will collect again and submit once more, and then it will be forever, forever. We will give them a quiet modest happiness.‚Äù

Herd instinct.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I wonder.

Yours,
Vernon.

V

Moscow.

I have found the other passage in Dostoevsky. I think it is the one you mean.

‚ÄúAnd we alone, we who guard the mystery, we alone shall be unhappy. There will be thousands of millions of happy children and only a hundred thousand martyrs who have taken on themselves the curse of good and evil.‚Äù

You mean, and Dostoevsky meant, that there must always be individualists. It is the individualists who carry on the torch. Men welded into a vast machine must ultimately perish. For the machine is soulless and will end as scrap iron.

Men worshipped stone and built Stonehenge‚ÅÝ‚Äîand today, the men who built it have perished and are unknown and Stonehenge stands. And yet, by a paradox, the men are alive in you and me, their descendants, and Stonehenge and what it stood for, is dead. The things that die endure, and the things that endure perish.

It is Man that goes on forever. (Does he? Isn‚Äôt that unwarrantable arrogance? Yet we believe it!) And so, there must be individualists behind the Machine. So Dostoevsky says and so you say. But then you‚Äôre both Russians. As an Englishman I‚Äôm more pessimistic.

Do you know what that passage from Dostoevsky reminds me of? My childhood. Mr.¬ÝGreen‚Äôs hundred children‚ÅÝ‚Äîand Poodle, Squirrel and Tree. Representatives of the hundred thousand.

Yours,
Vernon.

VI

Moscow.

Dear Sebastian:

I suppose you‚Äôre right. I never have thought much before. It seemed to me an unprofitable exercise. In fact, I‚Äôm not sure I don‚Äôt still regard it as such.

The trouble is, you see, that I can‚Äôt ‚Äúsay it in music.‚Äù Damn it all, why can‚Äôt I say it in music? Music‚Äôs my job. I‚Äôm more sure of that than ever. And yet‚ÅÝ‚Äînothing doing.

It‚Äôs hell.

Vernon.

VII

Dear Sebastian:

Haven‚Äôt I mentioned Jane? What is there to say about her? She‚Äôs splendid. We both know that. Why don‚Äôt you write to her yourself?

Yours ever,
Vernon.

VIII

Dear old Sebastian:

Jane says you may be coming out here. I wish to God you would. I‚Äôm sorry I haven‚Äôt written for six months‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI never was one of the world‚Äôs ready letter writers.

Have you seen anything of Joe? I‚Äôm glad Jane and I looked her up passing through Paris. Joe‚Äôs staunch, she‚Äôll never split on us, and I‚Äôm glad she at any rate knows. We never write to each other, she and I, we never have. But I wondered if you‚Äôd heard anything. I didn‚Äôt think she looked awfully fit. Poor old Joe! She‚Äôs made a mess of things.

Have you heard anything of Tatlin‚Äôs scheme for a monument to the Third Internationale? To consist of a union of three great glass chambers connected by a system of vertical axes and spirals. By means of special machinery they were to be kept in perpetual motion but at different rates of speed.

And inside, I suppose, they‚Äôd sing hymns to a Holy Acetylene Blowpipe!

Do you remember, one night, we were motoring back to town, and we took the wrong turning somewhere amongst the tramlines of Lewisham, and instead of making for the haunts of civilization we turned up somewhere among the Surrey docks and through an opening in the frowsy houses we saw a queer kind of cubist picture of cranes and cloudy steam and iron girders. And immediately your artistic soul bagged it for a drop scene‚ÅÝ‚Äîor whatever the technical term is.

My God, Sebastian! What a magnificent spectacle of machinery you could build up‚ÅÝ‚Äîsheer effects and lighting‚ÅÝ‚Äîand masses of humans with inhuman faces‚ÅÝ‚Äîmass‚ÅÝ‚Äînot individuals. You‚Äôve something of the kind in mind, haven‚Äôt you?

The architect, Tatlin, said something that I think good and yet a lot of nonsense.

‚ÄúOnly the rhythm of the metropolis, of factories and machines together with the organization of the masses, can give the impulse to the new art‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

And he goes on to speak of the ‚Äúmonument of the machine,‚Äù the only adequate expression of the present.

You know, of course, all about the modern Russian Theatre. That‚Äôs your job. I suppose Mayerhold is as marvellous as they say he is. But can one mix up drama and propaganda?

All the same, it‚Äôs exciting to arrive at a theatre and be compelled at once to join a marching crowd‚ÅÝ‚Äîup and down, in strict step, till the performance begins‚ÅÝ‚Äîand the scenery, composed of rocking chairs and cannons and revolving bays and God knows what! It‚Äôs babyish‚ÅÝ‚Äîabsurd‚ÅÝ‚Äîand yet one feels that baby has got hold of a dangerous and rather interesting toy that in other hands‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

Your hands, Sebastian‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou‚Äôre a Russian. But thank heaven and geography, no propagandist‚ÅÝ‚Äîjust a showman pure and simple‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

The Rhythm of the metropolis‚ÅÝ‚Äîmade pictorial.

My God!‚ÅÝ‚Äîif I could give you the music. It‚Äôs the music that‚Äôs needed.

Lord‚ÅÝ‚Äîtheir ‚Äúnoise orchestras‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Äîtheir symphonies of factory sirens! There was a show at Baku in 1922‚ÅÝ‚Äîbatteries of artillery, machine-guns, choirs, naval fog horns. Ridiculous! Yes, but if they had a composer‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

No woman ever longed for a child like I long to produce music.

And I‚Äôm barren‚ÅÝ‚Äîsterile.

Vernon.

IX

Dear Sebastian:

It seems like a dream, your having come and gone. Will you really do The Tale of the Rogue Who Outwitted Three Other Rogues? I wonder.

I‚Äôm only just beginning to recognize what a howling success you‚Äôve made of things. I‚Äôve at last grasped that you‚Äôre simply it nowadays. Yes, found your National Opera House‚ÅÝ‚ÄîGod knows it‚Äôs time we had one. But what do you want with opera? It‚Äôs archaic‚ÅÝ‚Äîdead‚ÅÝ‚Äîridiculous individual love affairs‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

Music up to now seems to me like a child‚Äôs drawing of a house‚ÅÝ‚Äîfour walls, a door, two windows and a chimney pot. There you are‚ÅÝ‚Äîand what more do you want!

At any rate Feinberg and Prokofiev do more than that.

Do you remember how we used to jeer at the cubists and futurists? At least I did. Now that I come to think of it, I don‚Äôt believe you agreed.

And then one day‚ÅÝ‚Äîat a cinema‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI saw a view of a big city from the air. Spires turning over, buildings bending‚ÅÝ‚Äîeverything behaving as one simply knew concrete and steel and iron couldn‚Äôt behave! And for the first time I got a glimmering of what old Einstein meant when he talked about relativity.

We don‚Äôt know anything about the shape of music, We don‚Äôt know anything about the shape of anything, for that matter‚ÅÝ‚Äîbecause there‚Äôs always one side open to space.

Some day you‚Äôll know what I mean‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhat music can mean‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhat I‚Äôve always known it meant.

What a mess that opera of mine was. All opera is a mess. Music was never intended to be representational. To take a story and write descriptive music to it is as wrong as to write a passage of music‚ÅÝ‚Äîin the abstract so to speak‚ÅÝ‚Äîand then find an instrument capable of playing it! When Stravinsky wrote a clarinet passage, you can‚Äôt even conceive of it as being played by anything else!

Music should be like mathematics‚ÅÝ‚Äîa pure science, untouched by drama, or romanticism, or any emotion other than the pure emotion which is the result of sound divorced from ideas.

I‚Äôve always known that in my heart. Music must be Absolute.

Not, of course, that I shall realize my ideal. To create pure sound untouched by ideas is a counsel of perfection.

My music will be the music of machinery. I leave the dressing of it to you. It‚Äôs an age of choreography, and choreography will reach heights we don‚Äôt as yet dream of. I can trust you with the visual side of my masterpiece as yet unwritten‚ÅÝ‚Äîand which in all probability never will be written.

Music must be four-dimensional‚ÅÝ‚Äîtimbre, pitch, relative speed and periodicity.

I don‚Äôt think even now we appreciate Sch√∂nberg enough. That clean remorseless logic that is the spirit of today. He and he alone had the courage to disregard tradition‚ÅÝ‚Äîto get down to bedrock, and discover Truth.

He‚Äôs the one man to my mind who matters. Even his scheme of score writing will have to be adopted universally. It‚Äôs absolutely necessary if scores are going to be intelligible.

The thing I have against him is his scorn of his instruments. He‚Äôs afraid of being a slave to them. He makes them serve him whether they will or no.

I‚Äôm going to glorify my instruments. I‚Äôm going to give them what they want‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhat they‚Äôve always wanted.

Damn it all, Sebastian, what is this strange thing, music? I know less and less.

Yours,
Vernon.

X

I know I haven‚Äôt written. I‚Äôve been busy. Making experiments. Means of expression for the Nameless Beast. In other words, instrument making. Metals are jolly interesting. I‚Äôm working with alloys just at present.

What a fascinating thing sound is.

Jane sends her love.

In answer to your question‚ÅÝ‚ÄîNo, I don‚Äôt suppose I shall ever leave Russia‚ÅÝ‚Äînot even to attend at your newly planned opera house, disguised in my beard!

It‚Äôs even more barbarous and beautiful now than when you saw it! Full and flowing, the perfect temperamental Slav Beaver!

But in spite of the forest camouflage, here I am and here I stay, till I am exterminated by one of the bands of wild children.

Yours ever,
Vernon.

Telegram from Vernon Deyre to Sebastian Levinne

Just heard Joe dangerously ill feared dying stranded in New York Jane and I sailing Resplendent hope see you London.
